6: al-pi shnayim edim o shloshah edim yumot hamet al-pi ed echad lo yumot (ʿal-pî šĕnayim ʿēdîm ʾô šĕlōšāh ʿēdîm yûmat hammēt ʿal-pî ʿēd ʾeḥād lōʾ yûmat): 'On the evidence of two witnesses or of three witnesses the one who is to die shall be put to death; a person shall not be put to death on the testimony of one witness.' The multiple-witness principle appears throughout Torah (Num 35:30; Deut 19:15) and is the evidentiary foundation of Israelite jurisprudence. Jesus applies it to church discipline (Matt 18:16: if he does not listen, take one or two others along, so that every charge may be established by the evidence of two or three witnesses). Paul cites the exact formula in 2 Cor 13:1 and 1 Tim 5:19, extending it to apostolic authority and elder accountability. John 8:17 has Jesus cite it to validate his own self-testimony: in your Law it is written that the testimony of two people is true; I am the one who bears witness about myself, and the Father bears witness about me — claiming divine corroboration that meets the Torah standard.
18: vehayah be'shivto al-kise mamlakhto vekatav lo et-mishneh hatorah hazot al-sefer milifnei hakohanim halevi'im (wĕhāyāh kĕšibtô ʿal kissēʾ mamlaktô wĕkātab lô ʾet-mišnēh hattôrāh hazzōʾt ʿal-sēp̄er miliprĕnê hakkōhănîm hallwîyîm): 'And when he sits on the throne of his kingdom, he shall write for himself in a book a copy of this law, approved by the Levitical priests.' Mishneh hatorah (copy/repetition of this law) — this phrase is often thought to be the Hebrew original behind the Greek deuteronomion, giving the book its Greek name Deuteronomy. The king must personally copy the Torah (katav lo, write for himself) and read it all the days of his life (v. 19) so that his heart is not lifted up above his brothers. No hereditary autocracy; the king is bound by the same covenant-word as every Israelite. The ideal king is therefore not the triumphant warrior but the Torah-student who rules under the law. Psalm 119 may be read as the meditation of such a king; and the NT presents Jesus as the one who fulfills the law (Matt 5:17) from inside it.
1: lo-tizbaḥ laYHWH Eloheicha shor vaseh asher yihyeh bo mum kol davar ra: 'You shall not sacrifice to YHWH your God an ox or a sheep that has a defect (mûm), any serious flaw (kol dāḇār rāʿ).' kî tôʿăḇat YHWH Eloheycha hûʾ (for it is an abomination (tôʿēḇāh) to YHWH your God) — defective sacrifice is not merely insufficient; it is abominable. Malachi 1:8 charges post-exilic Israel with precisely this: 'When you offer blind animals in sacrifice, is that not evil? When you offer those that are lame or sick, is that not evil?' The standard of unblemished sacrifice established here runs through Levitical law (Lev 22:20-25) to the typological fulfillment: Christ as the Passover lamb 'without blemish' (1 Pet 1:19).
2: ki-yimmatze veqirbkha be'aḥad she'areykha ish o-isha asher ya'aseh et-hara: 'If there is found among you, in any of your towns, a man or a woman who does evil in the eyes of YHWH your God.' The case law on apostasy (vv. 2-7) concerns those who ʿāḇar ʾet-bᵉrîtô (transgressed his covenant) — the verb ʿāḇar (to cross over, transgress) applied to covenant violation uses the crossing-over metaphor: apostasy is the crossing of the covenant boundary into the outside territory of other gods. The structural principle is that covenant violation is a capital matter; the community's holiness requires the removal of the apostate, not out of cruelty but because the covenant is a living relationship that apostasy poisons.
3: vayelekh vayya'avod elohim aḥerim vayishtaḥu lahem o lashemesh o layareaḥ: 'and has gone and served other gods and bowed down to them, or to the sun or the moon or any of the heavenly host.' The specific enumeration — other gods, sun, moon, heavenly host (tzᵉḇāʾ haššāmayim) — maps the full range of Canaanite-Mesopotamian celestial religion. Astral worship (sun, moon, stars) was the most cosmopolitan form of polytheism in the ancient world, practiced by Babylonians, Phoenicians, and Canaanites. ʾăšer lōʾ-ṣiwwîṯî (which I did not command) — the fundamental prohibition is not against any particular deity but against any divinity YHWH did not authorize: the covenant is exclusive, and any unauthorized deity-relationship is treachery.
4: vehugad lekha veshama'ta veddarashta heitev vehinne emet nakhon hadavar: 'and you are informed and hear about it, then you shall investigate diligently (darašta hêṭēḇ); and if it is true and confirmed.' dāraštā hêṭēḇ (you shall investigate well, diligently) — the due-process requirement: before capital punishment is applied, thorough investigation is mandatory. The concern for evidentiary certainty reflects the covenant's justice-theology: YHWH's law requires both the punishment of evil and protection against wrongful punishment. The subsequent witnesses-requirement (v. 6) reinforces this: capital punishment requires multiple eyewitnesses, not suspicion or accusation alone.
5: vehotzeta et-ha'ish hahu o et-ha'isha hahi el-she'arecha: 'then bring that man or woman to your town gate and stone them to death.' el-šaʿăreykā (to your town gate) — execution at the town gate (the community's public space of commerce, justice, and social life) makes the punishment a community act, not a private vendetta. The stoning itself distributes the act across the community: no single executioner bears the full weight; the community collectively enacts the covenant's defense of its own integrity. The witnesses act first (v. 7: yaḏ hāʿēḏîm tihyeh-bô bārʾišônāh), requiring those who testified to bear the primary responsibility for the death they testified to produce.
7: yad ha'edim tihyeh-bo barishona lahamito veyad kol-ha'am baaharon: 'The witnesses shall be first to put him to death, and then all the people shall follow.' The witnesses-first-to-act requirement binds testimony to consequence: the witnesses cannot hide behind procedural distance from the execution. This dual role (testifier and first executioner) raises the cost of false witness dramatically — you must be prepared to kill the person you are accusing. ûḇiʿartā hārāʿ miqqirbbekā (so you shall purge the evil from your midst) — the ûḇiʿartā hārāʿ formula (purge/burn out the evil) appears seven times in Deuteronomy (13:5; 17:7,12; 19:19; 21:21; 22:21,24; 24:7) as the covenant's self-cleansing mechanism.
8: ki yippale mimkha davar lamishpat bein-dam ledam bein-din ledin uvein-nega lanega: 'If a case arises that is too difficult for you, involving disputed homicide, disputed legal rights, or disputed assault.' The supreme court provision (vv. 8-13) addresses cases that exceed local judicial capacity. yippālēʾ mimkā (is too difficult/wonderful for you) — the same root (pālāʾ) as the divine wonders (Exod 3:20; 15:11); a case that is too wonderful for local courts is one that exceeds human capacity and requires higher authority. The three categories — dam lᵉḏām (blood-matter to blood-matter, homicide degrees), dîn lᵉḏîn (case to case, civil disputes), nega lᵉnegaʿ (wound to wound, assault degrees) — are the three principal case-types of ancient Israelite law.
9: uvata el-hakohanim hallevi'im ve'el-hashofet asher yihyeh bayamim hahem: 'You shall go to the Levitical priests and to the judge who is serving at that time, and you shall consult them.' The supreme court consists of Levitical priests (for the theological-cultic dimension of difficult cases) and a civil judge (šōpēṭ). The combination of priestly and judicial authority reflects the covenant's unified legal structure: law is not divided into sacred and secular. ʾăšer yihyeh bayyāmîm hāhēm (who is serving in those days) — the judge's authority is tied to his appointment, not to personal charisma or genealogy; the institution (the office) has authority, not merely the individual.
10: ve'asita al-pi hadavar asher yaggidu lekha min-hamakom hahu: 'You shall carry out the verdict they announce to you from that place YHWH will choose, and be careful to do according to all they instruct you.' The appellate verdict is binding. vᵉšāmartā laʿăśôt kᵉḵōl ʾăšer yôrûkā (be careful to do according to all they instruct you) — the verb yāḏāh (instruct) in Hiphil is the teaching-instruction form: the priests-and-judge's ruling is an act of Torah-instruction, not merely judicial decree. The supreme court's authority is derivative from Torah: they instruct (yōrûkā) what the Torah requires in the difficult case.
11: al-pi hatorah asher yorukha ve'al-hamishpat asher yomru lekha ta'aseh: 'According to the instruction they give you and the verdict they pronounce, you shall act. Do not deviate from the verdict they announce to you, to the right or to the left.' lo-tāsûr min-haddāḇār ʾăšer-yaggîḏû lᵉkā yāmîn ûśᵉmōl (do not deviate to the right or the left) — the right-left directional metaphor for exact compliance with Torah-instruction appears elsewhere in Deuteronomy (5:32; 28:14) and Joshua (1:7; 23:6): exact adherence to YHWH's word requires neither addition (right deviation) nor subtraction (left deviation).
12: veha'ish asher-ya'aseh bezadon leviltiy shemoa el-hakohen: 'The man who acts arrogantly by refusing to obey the priest who stands to minister before YHWH your God.' bezāḏôn (in arrogance, presumptuously) — the root zûḏ/zāḏôn (to be insolent, to act presumptuously) describes not merely disobedience but the posture of arrogance that places one's own judgment above authorized Torah-instruction. ûmēt hāʾîš hahûʾ ûḇiʿartā hārāʿ miyyiśrāʾēl (that man shall die; you shall purge the evil from Israel) — the death penalty for contempt of the supreme court is not about protecting judicial dignity but about preserving the covenant community's submission to YHWH's appointed instruction-authority.
13: vekhol-ha'am yishme'u veyira'u velo yezidun od: 'And all the people shall hear and be afraid, and they will not act presumptuously again.' wᵉyirāʾû wᵉlōʾ yᵉzîḏûn ʿôḏ (will fear and not act presumptuously again) — the public deterrence function of exemplary punishment is explicitly stated. Deuteronomy's criminal law consistently has this dual function: removing the offender (bîʿartā hārāʿ) and deterring others through fear. This deterrence-through-public-knowledge pattern recurs in Acts 5:11 after Ananias and Sapphira's deaths: 'great fear came upon the whole church and upon all who heard of these things.'
14: ki-tavo el-ha'aretz asher YHWH Eloheicha noten lakh vyrishtah veyashavta bah: 'When you come to the land YHWH your God is giving you and have taken possession and settled in it, and you say, Let me set a king over me like all the nations around me.' The law of the king (vv. 14-20) is introduced as a prediction of Israel's future desire: śîmāh ʿālay melek (set a king over me). The permissive-yet-regulated character of the royal law is distinctive: YHWH does not command a king but permits one with strict conditions. The phrase kᵉkol-haggôyim ʾăšer sᵉḇîḇōtay (like all the nations around me) — the explicit comparison to surrounding nations identifies the motivation as conformist: Israel wants a king not because of covenant need but to match international political norms. Samuel's rebuke in 1 Sam 8 draws directly on this passage.
15: som tasim alekha melekh asher yivḥar YHWH Eloheicha bo: 'you may set a king over you — but he must be the one YHWH your God chooses.' śôm tāśîm ʿāleykā melek (you shall certainly set a king over you) — the doubled imperative is permissive-mandatory: if Israel will have a king, then YHWH's selection process applies. mittqerev aḥeykha (from among your brothers) — the king must be a fellow Israelite, not a foreign appointee. The YHWH-chooses clause (repeated in 1 Sam 16:1: 'I have provided for myself a king among his sons') frames the entire monarchy under divine selection rather than human election or hereditary right. The Northern Kingdom's repeated violations of this principle (1 Kgs 12:16-20; Hos 8:4) reflect the ongoing tension between the YHWH-chooses model and human political reality.
16: raq lo-yarbeh-lo susim velo-yashiv et-ha'am mitzraymah: 'But he must not acquire many horses for himself or send people back to Egypt to get more horses.' The three anti-accumulation prohibitions — no horses, no wives, no silver/gold (vv. 16-17) — are the Law of the King's core restrictions. lo-yarbeh-lo susim (he shall not multiply horses for himself) — the reflexive lo (for himself) makes the prohibition self-interested accumulation, not military capacity per se. The Egypt-horse trade is explicitly prohibited: vᵉlōʾ yāšîḇ ʾet-hāʿām Miṣraymāh (he shall not return the people to Egypt) — the theological rationale is stated: 'The LORD has said to you, You shall never return that way again.' The Exodus is irreversible; any policy that recreates dependence on Egypt reverses the redemption.
17: velo yarbeh-lo nashim velo yasur levavo vekhesef vezahav lo yarbeh-lo meod: 'He must not take many wives for himself, so his heart is not led astray, nor shall he accumulate excessive silver and gold.' The three prohibitions are narrated precisely in Solomon's failure: he multiplied horses (1 Kgs 10:26-29), multiplied wives (1 Kgs 11:1-8: 700 wives and 300 concubines), and accumulated silver and gold (1 Kgs 10:14-22). wᵉlōʾ yāsûr lᵉḇāḇô (his heart shall not turn aside) — the heart-turning metaphor for covenant infidelity: the wives 'turned his heart after other gods' (1 Kgs 11:4). Deuteronomy's royal law is structured as a prophylactic against the very failures that the most celebrated king would exemplify — a programmatic critique embedded in the law before the fact.
19: vehayeta immo veqara vo kol-yemei ḥayav lema'an yilmad leyira et-YHWH Elohav: 'It shall be with him, and he shall read in it all the days of his life, that he may learn to fear YHWH his God.' The king's Torah-reading is for formation, not merely information: lᵉmaʿan yilmad lᵉyārᵉʾāh ʾet-YHWH Elohāyw (that he may learn to fear YHWH his God). The fear of YHWH (yirʾat YHWH) is the foundational disposition of covenant faithfulness — the comprehensive awe-and-trust that produces obedience. lišmōr ʾet-kol-diḇrê hattôrāh hazzōʾt wᵉʾet-haḥuqqîm hāʾēlleh (keeping all the words of this Torah and these statutes) — Torah-reading produces Torah-observance, but the causal sequence is formation through immersion (kol-yemê ḥayyāyw, all the days of his life): daily reading, lifelong formation.
20: leviltiy rum-levavo me'eḥav uviltiy sur min-hamitzvah yamim usmol: 'so that his heart will not be lifted up above his brothers, and so that he will not turn from the commandment to the right or to the left.' lᵉḇiltî rûm-lᵉḇāḇô mēʾeḥāyw (that his heart not be lifted above his brothers) — the king's danger is heart-elevation: social and political superiority translating into covenantal arrogance, the assumption that his role exempts him from the covenant demands binding on all Israelites. lᵉmaʿan yaʾărîk yāmîm ʿal-mamlaḵtô hûʾ ûḇānāyw bᵉqereb yiśrāʾēl (so that he may lengthen his days in his kingdom, he and his children, in the midst of Israel) — the long-dynasty promise is conditional on Torah-humility. The NT's servant-kingship theology (Mark 10:42-45: 'the greatest among you shall be your servant') fulfills this Deuteronomic vision of the king whose authority is expressed through covenant obedience and self-lowering, not elevation above the covenant community.
